planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; kepel fruit is a rare and legendary Javanese fruit tree associated with the royal court of the Yogyakarta Sultanate — the deep-brown, plum-sized cauliflorous fruits have the unusual property of making the sweat, breath, and urine of those who eat them smell like violets — and were traditionally reserved for the exclusive use of Javanese royalty.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; kepel fruit is slow-growing and best suited to zone 11–12 humid tropical conditions with consistent moisture. Stelechocarpus burahol (kepel fruit or burahol) is one of the world's most unusual and historically remarkable fruit trees — a medium-sized tropical tree in the custard apple family (Annonaceae) native to Java, Indonesia, where it has been deeply associated with the royal courts of Yogyakarta and Surakarta for centuries. The rounded, deep-brown, plum-sized fruits emerge cauliflourously in dense clusters on the trunk and main branches, and have a pleasant, sweet, melon-custard apple flavor. What makes kepel fruit uniquely extraordinary, however, is its documented effect on body chemistry: eating the fruits causes the sweat, breath, and urine of the consumer to take on a floral fragrance — reportedly reminiscent of violets or jasmine — for several hours. This effect was so prized in Javanese royal culture that kepel trees were traditionally planted exclusively in the gardens of the Kraton (royal palace), and common people were reportedly forbidden to grow or eat the fruit. The active compounds are thought to be terpenoids that are metabolized into aromatic volatiles excreted through the skin and kidneys. Kepel is extremely rare in cultivation outside Java; it is categorized as a vulnerable species due to limited habitat and restricted cultivation history.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Journal of Ethnobiology and Ethnomedicine — Javanese Royal Plants
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Unusual Tropical Fruits

subcategory: tropical Southeast Asian cauliflorous fruit tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; sweet rambai (Baccaurea dulcis) is a small tropical fruit tree from the lowland forests of Borneo and the Malay Peninsula, producing clusters of small, round, yellowish to orange-red, sweet, jelly-fleshed berries — cauliflorous, forming on the trunk and branches — the sweetest of the Baccaurea fruit species, with mild, sweet, pleasant flavor; dioecious (male and female plants needed for fruiting).

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; plant at least one male and one female tree for fruiting — Baccaurea dulcis is dioecious. Baccaurea dulcis (sweet rambai or jentik-jentik) is a small tropical fruit tree from the humid lowland forests of Borneo and the Malay Peninsula — one of the sweetest and most pleasant of the Baccaurea fruit species (the species name 'dulcis' means sweet in Latin). The tree is a compact size compared to other Baccaurea relatives, making it somewhat more suitable for home tropical gardens. Like other Baccaurea species, the fruits are cauliflorous — forming in pendant clusters directly on the trunk and main branches in the distinctive pattern shared with cacao, jackfruit, and related tropical trees. The small (1/2-inch), round to oval fruits ripen to yellowish to orange-red with thin skin and translucent, white, jelly-like flesh; the flavor is genuinely sweet and mild, with less of the tartness characteristic of other rambai relatives (B. motleyana). The tree is dioecious, requiring both male and female plants for fruit production. Sweet rambai is largely unknown in cultivation outside its native Borneo and Malaysian range; a rare collectors' fruit tree for dedicated tropical fruit enthusiasts in zone 11–12.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Baccaurea species
MARDI (Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic, loamy soil in full sun to part shade; mountain pepper (Litsea cubeba) is a small laurel-family tree from subtropical China, Taiwan, and Southeast Asia — the small, pepper-like fruits have an intensely lemon-floral, citrus-pepper aroma and are used as a spice and as one of the world's most important natural sources of citral, the primary compound in lemongrass oil, used in perfumery, flavoring, and as an antimicrobial.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic, loamy soil in full sun to part shade; litsea grows naturally as an understory tree in subtropical forest and tolerates partial shade well. Litsea cubeba (mountain pepper, fragrant litsea, or maychangberry) is a small to medium-sized deciduous to semi-evergreen tree in the laurel family (Lauraceae) native to the subtropical mountain forests of central and southern China, Taiwan, and Southeast Asia. The tree produces small (1/4-inch), round, pepper-like black fruits (cubeba = resembling cubeb pepper) with an extraordinarily intense, clean, lemon-floral-citrus aroma — one of the most concentrated natural sources of citral (60–80% of essential oil content), the same aromatic compound found in lemongrass (Cymbopogon), melissa, and other citrus-scented plants. The essential oil of L. cubeba (may chang oil or litsea oil) is extracted commercially and used extensively in high-end perfumery, soap, and cosmetic formulations, and as a natural food flavoring. The dried fruits can be used as a spice — adding a bright, lemony, floral-pepper note to dishes — in a culinary style similar to Sichuan pepper or pink peppercorn. In Taiwan and southern China, the fruits, leaves, and bark are used in traditional mountain tribal cuisine as a distinctive aromatic spice. The tree also has documented antimicrobial activity from citral. It performs well in zone 8–11 subtropical gardens as an aromatic specimen or food forest understory tree.
sources: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry — Litsea cubeba Essential Oil
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Chinese Prickly Ash (Flatspine Prickly Ash, Sichuan Pepper Relative)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to rocky soil in full sun; Chinese prickly ash (Zanthoxylum simulans) is a thorny deciduous shrub from eastern China — one of the Zanthoxylum species used as a source of Chinese Sichuan pepper-type spice, producing small, red, split seed husks with the characteristic numbing, floral-citrus, electric-tingling spice compound hydroxy-alpha-sanshool — used as a Sichuan pepper substitute and in traditional Chinese medicine.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; wear thick gloves when harvesting or pruning due to sharp thorns. Zanthoxylum simulans (Chinese prickly ash or flatspine prickly ash) is a thorny deciduous shrub from eastern and central China — one of several Zanthoxylum species used as sources of the Sichuan pepper-type spice in Chinese cooking, alongside the more commonly cultivated Z. bungeanum (Sichuan pepper) and Z. piperitum (Japanese sansho). Like its famous relatives, Z. simulans produces small, red, split-open seed husks (pericarps) after the black seed falls out; these husks contain hydroxy-alpha-sanshool and related sanshool compounds that cause the unique 'ma' (numbing-tingling) sensation of Sichuan cooking — a distinctive electric, oscillating numbness of the tongue and lips, combined with floral, citrus, and spicy aromatic notes. Z. simulans is more cold-hardy than the primary Sichuan pepper (Z. bungeanum) and is one of the species sometimes sold as 'Sichuan pepper' in the US market. The fresh husks, dried husks, and sometimes the young leaves are all used as aromatic seasonings. As a garden plant, it is an attractive, fast-growing thorny shrub with fragrant compound leaves that provides hedging function while producing a useful spice crop.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Sichuan Pepper Species
American Botanical Council — Sichuan Pepper and Relatives

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; red-fleshed durian (Durio kutejensis) is a wild durian species from the lowland dipterocarp forests of Borneo — producing smaller, less intensely odorous fruits than common durian (D. zibethinus) with bright red-orange to yellow flesh considered by many connoisseurs to be sweeter and more nuanced in flavor, without the overwhelming sulfurous aroma of commercial durian.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; requires genuinely tropical, humid, frost-free conditions; a collectors' tree for dedicated tropical fruit enthusiasts. Durio kutejensis (red-fleshed durian, durian merah, or kuning) is one of approximately 30 Durio species native to the forests of Borneo and the Malay Peninsula — and considered by many tropical fruit connoisseurs to produce superior-quality fruit to the common commercial durian (D. zibethinus). The fruits of D. kutejensis are smaller than commercial durian (typically 2–4 lbs) and covered with shorter, broader spines; the flesh is distinctively bright red-orange to golden-yellow — dramatically different from the pale cream-yellow flesh of common durian. The flavor is often described as richer, sweeter, more complex, and less sulfurous than D. zibethinus — with strong caramel, butterscotch, and tropical fruit notes without the overwhelming, polarizing odor that makes commercial durian controversial. The relative mildness of the aroma compared to D. zibethinus is one of the key reasons D. kutejensis is considered by Southeast Asian fruit lovers to be among the finest of all wild durian species. Wild animals in Borneo (orangutans, civets, bearded pigs) are major consumers and dispersers of durian fruits; D. kutejensis provides critical nutrition to Borneo wildlife. Extremely rare in cultivation outside Borneo.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Durio kutejensis
Sarawak Forestry Department — Wild Durian Species
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Durian Species

planting.note: Direct sow on surface of well-drained, very lean, sandy to loamy soil in full sun after soil warms to 70°F+; press seeds lightly into soil surface — do not cover, as seeds need light to germinate; kiss-me-quick is a sprawling tropical ornamental annual related to moss rose (P. grandiflora) but with distinctly hairy stems and small, vivid magenta-pink to rose-purple flowers opening only in morning sun — a classic groundcover for hot, dry, sandy soils in Gulf Coast and tropical gardens.

directions: Surface sow on very lean, well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; needs no fertilizer — excess fertility produces all foliage and few flowers; self-sows freely in tropical climates. Portulaca pilosa (kiss-me-quick, hairy purslane, or pink purslane) is a spreading, mat-forming tropical annual native to the Caribbean, tropical Americas, and warm parts of North America — a close relative of the popular ornamental moss rose (P. grandiflora) and the common edible purslane (P. oleracea). The plant covers itself in tiny, vivid, hot-pink to magenta, 5-petaled flowers from summer through fall, blooming prolifically throughout the growing season with each individual flower lasting only until midday (closing when cloud cover or heat arrives). The stems are covered with hairy, white, silky trichomes (the 'pilosa' = hairy, in the species name), giving the foliage a soft, silvery-white appearance. Like all Portulaca, it is extremely drought-tolerant, thrives in pure sand, and self-sows prolifically in tropical climates where it can naturalize and return annually. Kiss-me-quick is widely grown in Gulf Coast and tropical gardens as a carefree groundcover for hot, dry, difficult sites — sidewalk cracks, sandy berms, and rocky slopes — where more demanding plants fail. A cheerful, low-maintenance plant for challenging hot-dry spots.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Portulaca species
Tropical Plant Database — Florida Native Plants and Ornamentals
